news_summary: Progress is being made to enhance social benefits for EU mobile workers, while Indigenous leaders emphasize the vital connection between environmental and Indigenous health at the UN.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès sont réalisés pour améliorer les prestations sociales des travailleurs mobiles de l'UE, tandis que les dirigeants autochtones soulignent, à l'ONU, le lien essentiel qui existe entre l'environnement et la santé des populations autochtones.
news_summary_es: Se están logrando avances para mejorar las prestaciones sociales de los trabajadores móviles de la UE, mientras que los líderes indígenas destacan en la ONU el vínculo fundamental que existe entre el medio ambiente y la salud de los pueblos indígenas.

articles.title: Press release - Provisional deal to update social benefit rules for EU mobile workers
articles.summary: The agreed text seeks to clarify social security rules for EU workers who have moved to a different EU country, while fairly distributing obligations among member states.Committee on Employment and Social Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
A provisional agreement on modernised rules to coordinate national social security systems was reached between negotiators from the European Parliament and the Council on Wednesday. The new rules focus on facilitating labour mobility within the EU, while safeguarding workers’ social rights in cross-border situations.



If confirmed, the provisional agreement would update EU rules for unemployment benefits for mobile workers and for the export of unemployment benefits for jobseekers moving to another EU country. It would also clarify rules regarding long-term care and family benefits, and strengthen rules on the coordination and enforcement of social security for posted workers (i.e. sent by their employer to work in another EU country temporarily).



The text also tries to ensure a more equitable distribution of financial burden among EU-countries and provides for strengthened measures to identify and tackle fraud, such as letterbox companies.



Member states would also be able to cooperate better to ensure that workers have social security using a notification system, while employers would be able to access information and complete relevant documents online also via the Electronic Exchange of Social Security Information (EESSI).



Quote



Rapporteur Gabriele Bischoff (S&D, DE) said: “Today is a good day for millions of mobile workers. This reform is long overdue and we have taken an important step forward today on the basis of a difficult but necessary compromise. Nearly ten years after the Commission’s proposal, citizens will benefit from clearer and more predictable rules on the coordination of social security systems, including for unemployment, family and long-term care benefits. At the same time, we are strengthening cooperation between Member States to better prevent abuse and tackle social dumping. Free movement of workers is a fundamental right but it must go hand in hand with fair and enforceable rules.”



Next steps



Both the Parliament and the Council need to adopt the provisional agreement formally before the rules can enter into force.



Background: main principles on coordination of social security systems

Citizens are covered by the legislation in one country at a time and only pay contributions in one country only.

All EU citizens have the same rights and obligations independent if they are nationals or from another member state (principle of equal treatment or non-discrimination).

Previous periods of insurance, work or residence in other countries are taken into account when granting benefits.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a provisional agreement to modernize and clarify EU social security rules for mobile workers, ensuring fair distribution of obligations among member states and strengthening protections for workers' social rights. This has broad positive impact on millions of EU citizens by facilitating labor mobility, improving access to unemployment, family, and long-term care benefits, and combating fraud and social dumping. The story is focused, detailed, and significant for the general public across the EU.
articles.personality_title: EU agrees on updated social security rules for workers moving between countries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people in the European Union (EU) move to different countries to work. Each country has its own social security system, which includes benefits like unemployment pay, family support, and care for the elderly. Until now, the rules about how these benefits work across countries were unclear and sometimes unfair.

**What happened** – On Wednesday, the European Parliament and the Council reached a provisional agreement to update and improve these rules. The new rules make it easier for workers moving between EU countries to get benefits like unemployment and family support. They also set clearer guidelines for workers sent temporarily to another country by their employer. The agreement includes better ways for countries to share information and fight fraud.

**Impact** – This update is important because it helps millions of workers who live and work in different EU countries. It makes sure they get the social benefits they deserve without confusion. It also helps countries share the financial responsibility fairly and stops dishonest companies from taking advantage of the system.

**What's next step** – The provisional agreement still needs to be officially approved by both the European Parliament and the Council. Once approved, the new rules will start to protect workers and improve cooperation between countries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU has agreed on clearer, fairer social security rules to support workers who move between its countries and to prevent fraud.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'UE adopte des règles mises à jour sur la sécurité sociale pour les travailleurs mobiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes dans l'Union européenne (UE) déménagent dans différents pays pour travailler. Chaque pays a son propre système de sécurité sociale, qui comprend des prestations comme les allocations chômage, le soutien familial et les soins aux personnes âgées. Jusqu'à présent, les règles sur le fonctionnement de ces prestations entre pays étaient floues et parfois injustes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Mercredi, le Parlement européen et le Conseil ont conclu un accord provisoire pour mettre à jour et améliorer ces règles. Les nouvelles règles facilitent l'accès des travailleurs mobiles aux prestations chômage et familiales. Elles clarifient aussi les règles pour les travailleurs envoyés temporairement dans un autre pays par leur employeur. L'accord prévoit de meilleurs échanges d'informations entre pays et renforce la lutte contre la fraude.

**Impact** – Cette mise à jour est importante car elle aide des millions de travailleurs vivant et travaillant dans différents pays de l'UE. Elle garantit qu'ils reçoivent les prestations sociales auxquelles ils ont droit, sans confusion. Elle permet aussi une répartition équitable des coûts entre pays et empêche les entreprises malhonnêtes d'abuser du système.

**Prochaine étape** – L'accord provisoire doit encore être officiellement approuvé par le Parlement européen et le Conseil. Une fois adopté, les nouvelles règles protégeront mieux les travailleurs et amélioreront la coopération entre pays.

**Message clé en une phrase** – L'UE a convenu de règles de sécurité sociale plus claires et justes pour soutenir les travailleurs mobiles et lutter contre la fraude.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE acuerda nuevas reglas de seguridad social para trabajadores que se mueven entre países
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas en la Unión Europea (UE) se mudan a diferentes países para trabajar. Cada país tiene su propio sistema de seguridad social, que incluye beneficios como el desempleo, apoyo familiar y cuidado de personas mayores. Hasta ahora, las reglas sobre cómo funcionan estos beneficios entre países eran confusas y a veces injustas.

**Qué pasó** – El miércoles, el Parlamento Europeo y el Consejo llegaron a un acuerdo provisional para actualizar y mejorar estas reglas. Las nuevas normas facilitan que los trabajadores móviles obtengan beneficios como desempleo y apoyo familiar. También aclaran las reglas para los trabajadores enviados temporalmente a otro país por su empleador. El acuerdo incluye mejores formas para que los países compartan información y combatan el fraude.

**Impacto** – Esta actualización es importante porque ayuda a millones de trabajadores que viven y trabajan en diferentes países de la UE. Asegura que reciban los beneficios sociales que merecen sin confusión. También ayuda a que los países compartan la responsabilidad financiera de manera justa y evita que empresas deshonestas aprovechen el sistema.

**Próximo paso** – El acuerdo provisional aún debe ser aprobado oficialmente por el Parlamento Europeo y el Consejo. Una vez aprobado, las nuevas reglas comenzarán a proteger a los trabajadores y mejorar la cooperación entre países.

**Resumen en una frase** – La UE ha acordado reglas de seguridad social más claras y justas para apoyar a los trabajadores móviles y prevenir el fraude.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interlocking puzzle pieces shaped like European country outlines fitting together seamlessly over a soft map background, with glowing pathways connecting them symbolizing mobile workers’ movement, alongside stylized scales balancing coins and hearts to represent fair social benefits and cooperation among EU nations.

articles.title: Indigenous health can’t be separated from environmental health, leaders tell UN
articles.content:
This story is published through the Indigenous News Alliance.

On the second day of the United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, or UNPFII, experts called attention to the ways Indigenous health is deeply tied to nature and highlighted how health inequalities are compounded by environmental degradation, extractive activities, and climate change.

The forum’s focus on Indigenous health comes as a new study by former Permanent Forum member Geoffrey Roth, who is a Standing Rock Sioux descendant, argues that U.N. agencies’ fragmented approach — addressing health, environment, and land rights through separate mandates — has “consistently failed Indigenous Peoples.” The study, presented as the forum opened its 25th session, positions environmental degradation, climate change, and biodiversity loss not as external pressures but as “direct manifestations of injury” to Indigenous well-being.

“For Indigenous peoples, health is deeply tied to the health of the land,” said Roth. “It’s not just about access to clinics or medicine — it’s about clean water, healthy forests, traditional foods, and the ability to maintain cultural practices. When the environment is damaged — whether from mining, deforestation, pollution, or climate change — it directly affects people’s health.”

At the forum, many Indigenous leaders spoke out about how the growing environmental crises increase the urgency to address their impacts on Indigenous health. “Climate change is also another threat to our health,” said Minnie Grey, former executive director of the Nunavik Regional Board of Health and Social Services in northern Canada. “We are people of the Arctic: We need the ice, we need the snow, and we need the wildlife that depend on it. Our hunters and people rely on these animals that sustain our food systems and nutrition.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

A second study, also presented at the forum by former Permanent Forum members Hanieh Moghani, Hannah McGlade, and Geoffrey Roth, examines how armed conflicts disproportionately affect Indigenous peoples, as they are frequently driven by competition over natural resources. This leads to the displacement of Indigenous peoples from ancestral lands and territories, the erosion of social and cultural cohesion, resource exploitation, and disruptions to agricultural livelihoods, leading to intergenerational health crises.

“These impacts add to existing inequalities, which is why Indigenous communities are often hit hardest,” he explained. “In that sense, environmental damage isn’t separate from health — it’s a major driver of it.”

By focusing on Indigenous health as separate from territories, waters, food systems, and culture, Roth said global health efforts have failed to address the structural drivers of health problems Indigenous peoples face, such as land dispossession, environmental degradation, cultural disruption, and the erosion of Indigenous governance.

Geoffrey Roth speaks at UNPFII.

Carrie Johnson / Grist

The study emphasizes that climate change functions as a severe “risk multiplier” that intensifies pressures across biological, ecological, and social systems, with disproportionate impacts on Indigenous populations. Extreme weather events, such as droughts and flooding, degrade water quality and availability, which raises the risk of waterborne diseases and undermines hygiene. Furthermore, the climate crisis is driving severe mental health consequences in Indigenous communities. Evidence links climate-related disasters and environmental loss to increased rates of depression, substance abuse, and emerging diagnoses like “ecological grief” and “climate anxiety,” particularly among Indigenous youth who are watching their ancestral ecosystems change.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

In Alaska, for example, severe storm events like Typhoon Halong have devastated coastal villages, resulting in the forced climate relocation of thousands of Indigenous people. These relocations, driven by coastal erosion and thawing permafrost, cut communities off from their traditional food harvesting and weather forecasting systems, compounding their health vulnerabilities.

Biodiversity degradation, for instance, can impact food availability and therefore cause nutritional inequalities, chronic disease, and mental distress. In the Munduruku territory in Brazil, which is one of the lands that has been hardest hit by illegal mining in the country, the Indigenous Munduruku people face many health issues, even after a government-led operation to halt illegal mining in the territory.

Community members have reported a wide range of diseases linked to mercury pollution and ecological destruction caused by mining, including diarrhea, itchiness, flu, fever, childhood paralysis, and brain problems.

“The situation is even more serious for Indigenous peoples in voluntary isolation and initial contact,” said Ginny Alba Medina, an Indigenous leader and lawyer from OPIAC, the national organization for Colombia’s Amazon peoples. “For them, the right to health begins with absolute respect for the principle of no contact. Any external intrusion can trigger lethal epidemics against which they have no immune defenses. Allowing extractive activities, armed presence, or territorial pressure in their territories poses an immediate threat to their physical and cultural survival.”

“What’s been missing is a more connected approach — one that includes land, culture, and self-determination as central to health,” Roth said. “Moving forward, U.N. agencies need to reduce fragmentation and work in a more coordinated way. You cannot improve Indigenous health in isolation. It requires aligning efforts across sectors and supporting Indigenous leadership within these systems.”

Just weeks before the forum kicked off at the U.N. headquarters in New York, Indigenous Batwa women and children in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, or DRC, suffered fresh attacks by a group of armed men believed to belong to the Alliance Fleuve Congo and March 23 movement rebel groups. These cases, which took place on March 5 in the country’s South Kivu province, are linked to a larger pattern of targeted violence against the Batwa people to gain control over their land and natural resources.

Conflicts in Indigenous territories are inherently an environmental and health crisis. As armed conflicts are frequently driven by competition over natural resources, Indigenous lands become strategic battlegrounds.

Analysts have pointed out that this escalating armed conflict in the DRC has had a significant and often overlooked impact on the environment. They highlight a sharp increase in deforestation since it broke out in late 2021, with an estimated 3,019 acres of tree cover lost in 2023. Between 2019 and 2022, the yearly average forest loss was 1,410 acres.

Advocates at the conference discussed how conflict can restrict Indigenous peoples’ access to their lands, as they often must flee violence to protect themselves. But without access to their lands, similar to biodiversity degradation, which is sometimes also generated by conflict, Indigenous communities may struggle to obtain access to nutritional foods, leading to health impacts and the weakening of social and spiritual cohesion, as Roth’s study on Indigenous health pointed out.

“These conflicts have immediate and long-term health impacts,” Roth said. “Communities are displaced from their lands, access to healthcare is disrupted, and people face lasting trauma and stress. At the same time, the environment is damaged or destroyed — polluted water, deforestation, loss of food systems — which further undermines health.”

This is the situation Ngāti Tīpā peoples of Waikato Tainui Tribal Confederation in Tauranganui Marae, New Zealand, are facing.

“My great-great-grandmother said all the waters surrounding our community were once clean,” said Em-Haley Kūkūtai Walker, who is Ngāti Tīpā and an artist from the community. “We didn’t receive many floods, and our fisheries were healthy. Now, because of sea level rise into our river that is increasing salinity levels, fish are dying and moving elsewhere.”

Indigenous leaders at the forum, such as Wilton Littlechild, a Cree chief and lawyer defending treaty rights, argued that legal recognition for their territories is a foundational prerequisite to protect biodiversity and Indigenous health.

“Indigenous people have these treaties [and there is the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples], which are tools to protect their health,” said Littlechild.

This call was echoed by the WHO in its draft Global Plan of Action, or GPA, on the health of Indigenous people, which called for supporting “Indigenous-led ecosystem stewardship and nature-based approaches that safeguard health.” On February 5, the WHO’s Executive Board decided to delay consideration of the GPA draft to 2027 to allow more time for consultation.

According to advocates, Indigenous health is completely inseparable from land tenure, biodiversity, food sovereignty, and self-determination, and this must be recognized by bodies such as the U.N. and the WHO.

Leaders warn that global climate and biodiversity goals cannot be met without Indigenous peoples. In a session about tying national obligations under the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, or UNDRIP, to health, Ruth Mercredi, an elder and a traditional healer in Yellowknife, Canada, said governments need to start prioritizing Indigenous health.

“Today, we are getting sick of the water, of the food, of the air,” said Mercredi. “Whatever we are putting in our bodies. We have to now be mindful of that when we didn’t have to before.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the critical connection between Indigenous health and environmental health, emphasizing the need for integrated approaches to address systemic inequalities and environmental degradation impacting Indigenous peoples globally. It reports on significant advocacy at the UN level, calls for coordinated policy action, and stresses Indigenous leadership and rights, which can lead to meaningful improvements in health, cultural preservation, and environmental stewardship for large Indigenous populations worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Indigenous health linked to environment in urgent UN call for coordinated action
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – At the United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues (UNPFII), experts and Indigenous leaders gathered to discuss how the health of Indigenous peoples is closely connected to the health of their environment. They highlighted problems caused by environmental damage, climate change, and conflicts over land.

**What happened** – During the forum’s 25th session, a study by Geoffrey Roth, a former forum member and Standing Rock Sioux descendant, was presented. It showed that UN agencies often treat health, environment, and land rights separately, which has failed Indigenous communities. Indigenous leaders shared examples from places like Alaska, Canada, Brazil, and New Zealand, showing how pollution, climate change, and violence harm their health and culture.

**Impact** – This connection between environmental damage and Indigenous health is important because it explains why many Indigenous communities face serious health problems. For example, mercury pollution from illegal mining in Brazil causes diseases, while climate change in the Arctic threatens traditional food and mental health. The study also showed that conflicts over natural resources force Indigenous people from their homes, breaking their connection to the land and worsening health inequalities.

**What’s next step** – Experts and leaders are calling for UN agencies to work together instead of separately, supporting Indigenous leadership and respecting their rights to land and culture. The World Health Organization (WHO) has proposed a global plan to support Indigenous health through protecting ecosystems, but it will be reviewed again in 2027 to allow for more input. Indigenous voices want governments to recognize that protecting the environment is key to improving health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Indigenous health depends on a healthy environment, so protecting land, culture, and ecosystems together is essential to improving wellbeing and addressing inequalities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Santé des peuples autochtones et environnement : un appel urgent à l’action coordonnée à l’ONU
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lors du Forum permanent des Nations unies sur les questions autochtones (UNPFII), des experts et des dirigeants autochtones ont souligné le lien étroit entre la santé des peuples autochtones et celle de leur environnement. Ils ont mis en lumière les problèmes causés par la dégradation environnementale, le changement climatique et les conflits fonciers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de la 25e session du forum, une étude de Geoffrey Roth, ancien membre du forum et descendant Sioux de Standing Rock, a été présentée. Elle montre que les agences de l’ONU traitent souvent séparément la santé, l’environnement et les droits fonciers, ce qui a échoué auprès des communautés autochtones. Des leaders autochtones ont partagé des exemples d’Alaska, du Canada, du Brésil et de Nouvelle-Zélande, montrant comment la pollution, le changement climatique et la violence affectent leur santé et leur culture.

**Impact** – Ce lien entre la dégradation de l’environnement et la santé autochtone est important car il explique pourquoi de nombreuses communautés autochtones souffrent de graves problèmes de santé. Par exemple, la pollution au mercure due à l’exploitation minière illégale au Brésil cause des maladies, tandis que le changement climatique dans l’Arctique menace la nourriture traditionnelle et la santé mentale. L’étude montre aussi que les conflits liés aux ressources naturelles obligent les autochtones à quitter leurs terres, rompant leur lien avec celles-ci et aggravant les inégalités de santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts et dirigeants demandent aux agences de l’ONU de travailler ensemble, de soutenir le leadership autochtone et de respecter leurs droits à la terre et à la culture. L’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) a proposé un plan mondial pour soutenir la santé autochtone en protégeant les écosystèmes, mais il sera réexaminé en 2027 pour permettre plus de consultations. Les voix autochtones veulent que les gouvernements reconnaissent que protéger l’environnement est essentiel pour améliorer la santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La santé des peuples autochtones dépend d’un environnement sain, donc protéger ensemble leurs terres, leur culture et leurs écosystèmes est essentiel pour améliorer leur bien-être et réduire les inégalités.
articles.personality_title_es: Salud indígena y medio ambiente: llamado urgente de la ONU a la acción coordinada
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el Foro Permanente de las Naciones Unidas sobre Cuestiones Indígenas (UNPFII), expertos y líderes indígenas destacaron la estrecha relación entre la salud de los pueblos indígenas y la salud del medio ambiente. Señalaron problemas causados por la degradación ambiental, el cambio climático y los conflictos por tierras.

**Qué pasó** – Durante la 25ª sesión del foro, se presentó un estudio de Geoffrey Roth, exmiembro del foro y descendiente Sioux de Standing Rock. El estudio mostró que las agencias de la ONU suelen tratar la salud, el medio ambiente y los derechos de la tierra por separado, lo que ha fallado a las comunidades indígenas. Líderes indígenas compartieron ejemplos de Alaska, Canadá, Brasil y Nueva Zelanda, mostrando cómo la contaminación, el cambio climático y la violencia afectan su salud y cultura.

**Impacto** – Esta conexión entre el daño ambiental y la salud indígena es importante porque explica por qué muchas comunidades indígenas enfrentan graves problemas de salud. Por ejemplo, la contaminación por mercurio por minería ilegal en Brasil causa enfermedades, mientras que el cambio climático en el Ártico amenaza la alimentación tradicional y la salud mental. El estudio también mostró que los conflictos por recursos naturales obligan a los indígenas a abandonar sus tierras, rompiendo su vínculo con ellas y empeorando las desigualdades en salud.

**Próximo paso** – Expertos y líderes piden que las agencias de la ONU trabajen juntas, apoyen el liderazgo indígena y respeten sus derechos a la tierra y la cultura. La Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) propuso un plan global para apoyar la salud indígena mediante la protección de los ecosistemas, pero será revisado en 2027 para permitir más consultas. Las voces indígenas quieren que los gobiernos reconozcan que proteger el medio ambiente es clave para mejorar la salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – La salud indígena depende de un ambiente sano, por lo que proteger juntos sus tierras, cultura y ecosistemas es esencial para mejorar su bienestar y reducir desigualdades.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, detailed painting of a vibrant Indigenous village nestled beside a pristine river with clear water flowing through lush forests, where symbolic silhouettes of traditional fishing tools and wildlife native to Arctic and Amazon regions gently blend into the landscape, while subtle, warm light connects the land, water, and community in a harmonious circle representing the inseparable bond between Indigenous health, environment, and cultural resilience.
